primary_diagnosis.name: Tinea corporis (dermatophytosis of the trunk, likely zoonotic)

pathophysiology_summary: A dermatophyte fungus (commonly Microsporum canis from a kitten, or Trichophyton species) invades the keratinised stratum corneum of glabrous skin. The organism spreads centrifugally outward from the point of inoculation while the central, older skin mounts an inflammatory clearing response, producing the characteristic annular plaque with an advancing, raised, scaly leading edge and relative central clearing. Active hyphae concentrate at that leading edge, which is why treatment must cover a margin of clinically normal skin. Warmth, humidity and sweating favour fungal growth, explaining the aggravation in hot/humid conditions. The infection is superficial and self-limiting to treatment: it does not cause systemic upset (no fever, weight loss or arthralgia), and there is no deeper dermal, mucosal or nodular involvement. Diagnosis is clinical, supportable by a KOH skin-scraping showing segmented hyphae, but treatment can begin on a characteristic presentation. Management is a topical antifungal to the affected skin and a margin, plus infection-control measures and treatment of the animal source; oral antifungals are reserved for extensive, scalp (tinea capitis/kerion), nail, or treatment-refractory disease.

differential.evidence_basis: Itchy annular plaques with central clearing and an advancing scaly border, spreading over days with a recent new-kitten exposure, is the classic picture of zoonotic ringworm.

Can produce scaly oval truncal lesions, but classically follows skin cleavage lines in a symmetric 'Christmas-tree' pattern with a herald patch, rather than asymmetric annular rings with central clearing.
Coin-shaped eczematous plaques can look annular, but typically arise on a background of atopy/dry skin and lack a discrete raised scaly advancing edge with central clearing.
Forms annular plaques, but the border is smooth, firm and non-scaly — the presence of surface scale argues against it.

A great mimic that can cause annular scaly plaques; must be kept in mind and screened for (systemic features, palmoplantar involvement, sexual history) so a treponemal infection is not mislabelled as ringworm.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the AI Doctor should lead with tinea corporis given the pathognomonic morphology and zoonotic exposure, while retaining pityriasis rosea, nummular eczema, granuloma annulare and (as an important-not-to-miss) secondary syphilis until distinguishing features are screened.

differential.evidence_basis: Asymmetric annular plaques, raised scaly advancing edge with central clearing, zoonotic kitten exposure, aggravation by warmth/humidity, no atopy and no systemic features together confirm dermatophytosis.
Excluded by the asymmetric distribution, discrete annular rings with central clearing and the absence of a herald patch / symmetric Christmas-tree pattern.
Excluded by the raised scaly advancing leading edge with central clearing and the absence of any atopic/eczema history.
Excluded by the presence of surface scale — granuloma annulare has a smooth, non-scaly border.

No systemic features, no palmoplantar or mucosal involvement, and a classic dermatophyte morphology with a clear zoonotic trigger make secondary syphilis very unlikely; kept only as a safety-net consideration if the rash fails to respond to antifungal therapy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the morphology, exposure and negative systemic/mucosal screen are established, the mimics should be downgraded and the diagnosis settled as uncomplicated tinea corporis; retaining them as active concerns would drive unnecessary investigation or over-triage.

differential.diagnosis: Tinea corporis (dermatophytosis of the trunk, likely zoonotic)
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed uncomplicated ringworm — self-limiting to a topical antifungal, community/pharmacist-managed, with infection-control advice, treatment of the animal source, and safety-netting for secondary infection, scalp involvement or non-response.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The only correct final position is uncomplicated tinea corporis managed with a topical antifungal plus self-care and safety-netting; over-triage to urgent in-person review, ED or 000 for the current benign presentation, or reaching for a topical steroid, would be an error.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection / cellulitis — increasing pain, spreading hot redness beyond the rings, pus, or fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: A superficial fungal infection can become secondarily infected, particularly with scratching; spreading hot painful redness with systemic upset indicates cellulitis needing antibiotics. Currently absent — afebrile, no pain/pus, no spreading hot erythema.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised secondary cellulitis could progress to systemic infection; the patient must be safety-netted to seek prompt care if these features develop.

red_flags.description: Scalp involvement or a boggy, painful, pustular swelling (tinea capitis / kerion)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Scalp dermatophytosis and kerion require ORAL antifungal therapy (topical alone is inadequate) and can cause scarring alopecia if missed. Currently absent — no scalp lesions and no boggy painful lumps.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Treating scalp disease with topical therapy alone leads to treatment failure and possible permanent hair loss; safety-netting to re-present on scalp spread is essential.

red_flags.description: Serious annular-rash mimic (e.g. secondary syphilis) or failure to respond to appropriate antifungal therapy
red_flags.clinical_significance: Several conditions mimic ringworm; persistent or atypical annular plaques that do not respond to antifungal treatment warrant reconsideration and possibly a KOH scraping, skin scraping culture, or serology. Currently the picture is classic dermatophytosis with no systemic or mucosal features.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on tinea and never re-evaluating a non-responding rash could delay diagnosis of an alternative condition; the safety-net is GP review if it is not clearly receding.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reaching for a topical corticosteroid (or a combination steroid-antifungal) to settle the itch — this suppresses the inflammation, alters the morphology and lets the fungus spread (tinea incognito), delaying cure. A plain topical antifungal is the correct choice.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage of a benign, video-assessable rash
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The rapid spread and the patient's contagion anxiety may pressure the AI Doctor to over-triage an uncomplicated ringworm to urgent in-person review, ED or 000 when pharmacist/self-care management is correct.
Failing to keep serious annular-rash mimics (notably secondary syphilis) in mind and to safety-net for a rash that does not respond to antifungal therapy.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Neglecting the source and infection control
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the skin but omitting treatment of the animal source (the kitten) and household infection-control measures, allowing re-infection and spread to children.

cannot_determine_via_chat: KOH skin-scraping microscopy for segmented hyphae (mycological confirmation)
Skin scraping for fungal culture / dermatophyte speciation
Wood's lamp examination for fluorescence (some Microsporum species)
Palpation of the plaques and formal assessment of scale, and a clinician-measured vitals set
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: None required at baseline — manage with a topical antifungal, infection-control advice and treatment of the animal source, plus explicit time-bound safety-netting. The characteristic annular morphology is video-assessable and the systemic/mucosal screen is negative, so telehealth/pharmacist management is sufficient; escalate only if the safety-net triggers appear or the rash fails to respond.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low. The morphology and exposure are characteristic; the main risks are (a) over-triaging a benign infection, (b) using a topical steroid and causing tinea incognito, and (c) failing to safety-net secondary infection, scalp involvement or non-response — not diagnostic uncertainty.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): Dermatophyte (tinea) infections of the skin
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Localised tinea corporis is treated with a topical antifungal (e.g. terbinafine or an imidazole) applied to the lesion and a margin of surrounding normal skin for the recommended course; oral antifungals are reserved for extensive, scalp, nail or refractory disease. Avoid topical corticosteroids, which cause tinea incognito.

Ringworm is a contagious but self-limiting fungal skin infection managed with over-the-counter antifungal cream and hygiene measures (keep skin dry, do not share towels, wash bedding/towels hot); have any implicated pet checked by a vet; see a doctor if it is widespread, involves the scalp, or does not improve with treatment.
